subject: Math
description:
Build an understanding that a function from one set (called the domain) to another set (called the range) assigns to each element of the domain exactly one element of the range by recognizing that:
  • if f is a function and x is an element of its domain, then f(x) denotes the output of f corresponding to the input x.
  • the graph of f is the graph of the equation y = f(x).
description_plain: Your child understands that a function pairs each input with exactly one output and knows that the graph of a function shows this relationship.

  • Apply geometric concepts to solve design and optimization problems.

description:
Prove theorems about parallelograms.
  • Opposite sides of a parallelogram are congruent.
  • Opposite angles of a parallelogram are congruent.

subject: Math
description:
Prove theorems about triangles and use them to prove relationships in geometric figures including:
  • The sum of the measures of the interior angles of a triangle is 180º.
  • An exterior angle of a triangle is equal to the sum of its remote interior angles.
  • The base angles of an isosceles triangle are congruent.
  • The segment joining the midpoints of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and half the length.
description_plain: Your child can prove important facts about triangles, like their angles add up to 180º, exterior angles equal certain interior angles, base angles in isosceles triangles are equal, and the segment connecting midpoints of two sides is parallel and half the length of the third side.

subject: Math
description:
Prove theorems about lines and angles and use them to prove relationships in geometric figures including:
  • Vertical angles are congruent.
  • When a transversal crosses parallel lines, alternate interior angles are congruent.
  • When a transversal crosses parallel lines, corresponding angles are congruent.
  • Points are on a perpendicular bisector of a line segment if and only if they are equidistant from the endpoints of the segment.
  • Use congruent triangles to justify why the bisector of an angle is equidistant from the sides of the angle.
description_plain: Your child can prove facts about lines and angles, such as vertical angles being equal, angles formed by parallel lines and a transversal, and how points relate to perpendicular bisectors and angle bisectors.

subject: Math
description:
Experiment with transformations in the plane.
  • Represent transformations in the plane.
  • Compare rigid motions that preserve distance and angle measure (translations, reflections, rotations) to transformations that do not preserve both distance and angle measure (e.g. stretches, dilations).
  • Understand that rigid motions produce congruent figures while dilations produce similar figures.
description_plain: Your child can try out different ways to move and change shapes, understand which movements keep size and angles the same, and know that some changes make shapes similar but not exactly the same.

description:
Use the properties of rational and irrational numbers to explain why:
  • the sum or product of two rational numbers is rational;
  • the sum of a rational number and an irrational number is irrational;
  • the product of a nonzero rational number and an irrational number is irrational.
description_plain: Your child can explain why adding or multiplying two rational numbers gives a rational number, why adding a rational and an irrational number gives an irrational number, and why multiplying a nonzero rational number by an irrational number gives an irrational number.

subject: Math
description:
Solve word problems involving:
  • Quarters, dimes, nickels, and pennies within 99¢, using ¢ symbols appropriately.
  • Whole dollar amounts, using the $ symbol appropriately.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems using coins (quarters, dimes, nickels, pennies) up to 99 cents and whole dollar amounts, using the correct symbols for cents and dollars.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time from analog and digital clocks to the nearest five minutes, using a.m. and p.m.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the nearest five minutes on both analog and digital clocks, using a.m. and p.m.

subject: Math
description: Represent whole numbers as lengths from 0 on a number line diagram with equally spaced points and represent whole-number sums and differences, within 100, on a number line.
description_plain: Your child can show whole numbers as points on a number line and use the number line to add and subtract numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction, within 100, to solve word problems involving lengths that are given in the same units, using equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems about lengths using addition and subtraction within 100, and write equations with a symbol for the unknown number.

subject: Math
description: Measure to determine how much longer one object is than another, expressing the length difference in terms of a standard length unit.
description_plain: Your child can measure how much longer one object is than another and say the difference using standard units like inches or centimeters.

subject: Math
description: Estimate lengths in using standard units of inches, feet, yards, centimeters, and meters.
description_plain: Your child can estimate lengths using common units like inches, feet, yards, centimeters, and meters.

subject: Math
description: Measure the length of an object twice, using length units of different lengths for the two measurements; describe how the two measurements relate to the size of the unit chosen.
description_plain: Your child can measure the same object twice using different units and explain how the size of the unit changes the measurement.

subject: Math
description: Measure the length of an object in standard units by selecting and using appropriate tools such as rulers, yardsticks, meter sticks, and measuring tapes.
description_plain: Your child can measure the length of objects using tools like rulers, yardsticks, meter sticks, or measuring tapes.

subject: Math
description:
Organize, represent, and interpret data with up to four categories.
  • Draw a picture graph and a bar graph with a single-unit scale to represent a data set.
  • Solve simple put-together, take-apart, and compare problems using information presented in a picture and a bar graph.
description_plain: Your child can collect data with up to four groups, make picture and bar graphs with one-to-one scales, and solve simple problems by adding, subtracting, or comparing the data.

subject: Math
description: Mentally add 10 or 100 to a given number 100–900, and mentally subtract 10 or 100 from a given number 100–900.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add or subtract 10 or 100 to numbers between 100 and 900 in their head.

subject: Math
description:
Add and subtract, within 1,000, relating the strategy to a written method, using:
  • Concrete models or drawings
  • Strategies based on place value
  • Properties of operations
  • Relationship between addition and subtraction
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 1,000 using drawings, place value strategies, properties of operations, and understanding how addition and subtraction relate.

subject: Math
description: Add up to three two-digit numbers using strategies based on place value and properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can add up to three two-digit numbers using place value and math properties.

subject: Math
description:
Demonstrate fluency with addition and subtraction, within 100, by:
  • Flexibly using strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction.
  • Comparing addition and subtraction strategies, and explaining why they work.
  • Selecting an appropriate strategy in order to efficiently compute sums and differences.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract within 100 using different strategies, explain why they work, and choose the best way to solve problems quickly.

subject: Math
description: Compare two three-digit numbers based on the value of the hundreds, tens, and ones digits, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can compare two three-digit numbers by looking at the hundreds, tens, and ones digits and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description: Read and write numbers, within 1,000, using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form.
description_plain: Your child can read and write numbers up to 1,000 using digits, words, and expanded form.

subject: Math
description: Count within 1,000; skip-count by 5s, 10s, and 100s.
description_plain: Your child can count up to 1,000 and skip-count by 5s, 10s, and 100s.

subject: Math
description:
Understand that the three digits of a three-digit number represent amounts of hundreds, tens, and ones.
  • Unitize by making a hundred from a collection of ten tens.
  • Demonstrate that the numbers 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900 refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine hundreds, with 0 tens and 0 ones.
  • Compose and decompose numbers using various groupings of hundreds, tens, and ones.
description_plain: Your child understands that in a three-digit number, the digits show how many hundreds, tens, and ones there are, can group tens into hundreds, and can break apart and put together numbers in different ways.

subject: Math
description: Use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rectangular arrays with up to 5 rows and up to 5 columns; write an equation to express the total as a sum of equal addends.
description_plain: Your child can use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rows and columns (up to 5 rows and 5 columns) and write an equation showing the total as repeated addition.

subject: Math
description:
Determine whether a group of objects, within 20, has an odd or even number of members by:
  • Pairing objects, then counting them by 2s.
  • Determining whether objects can be placed into two equal groups.
  • Writing an equation to express an even number as a sum of two equal addends.
description_plain: Your child can tell if a group of up to 20 objects is odd or even by pairing them, counting by twos, and writing an equation showing even numbers as two equal groups.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate fluency with addition and subtraction, within 20, using mental strategies.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers within 20 using mental math.

subject: Math
description:
Represent and solve addition and subtraction word problems, within 100, with unknowns in all positions, by using representations and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem, when solving:
  • One-Step problems:
    • Add to/Take from-Start Unknown
    • Compare-Bigger Unknown
    • Compare-Smaller Unknown
  • Two-Step problems involving single digits:
    • Add to/Take from- Change Unknown
    • Add to/Take From- Result Unknown
description_plain: Your child can solve addition and subtraction word problems within 100, including one- and two-step problems, by using pictures, equations, and symbols for unknown numbers.

subject: Math

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Solve one-step word problems involving division of unit fractions by non-zero whole numbers and division of whole numbers by unit fractions using area and length models, and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that involve dividing unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions using pictures, models, and equations.

subject: Math
description:
Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction or whole number by a fraction, including mixed numbers.
  • Use area and length models to multiply two fractions, with the denominators 2, 3, 4.
  • Explain why multiplying a given number by a fraction greater than 1 results in a product greater than the given number and when multiplying a given number by a fraction less than 1 results in a product smaller than the given number.
  • Solve one-step word problems involving multiplication of fractions using models to develop the algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can multiply fractions and whole numbers by fractions, including mixed numbers, using area and length models, explain why multiplying by fractions changes the size of the number, and solve one-step word problems.

subject: Math
description:
Use fractions to model and solve division problems.
  • Interpret a fraction as an equal sharing context, where a quantity is divided into equal parts.
  • Model and interpret a fraction as the division of the numerator by the denominator.
  • Solve one-step word problems involving division of whole numbers leading to answers in the form of fractions and mixed numbers, with denominators of 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, and 12, using area, length, and set models or equations.
description_plain: Your child can use fractions to show and solve division problems by sharing equally, understand a fraction as dividing the top number by the bottom number, and solve word problems with answers as fractions or mixed numbers.

subject: Math
description:
Add and subtract fractions, including mixed numbers, with unlike denominators using related fractions: halves, fourths and eighths; thirds, sixths, and twelfths; fifths, tenths, and hundredths.
  • Use benchmark fractions and number sense of fractions to estimate mentally and assess the reasonableness of answers.
  • Solve one- and two-step word problems in context using area and length models to develop the algorithm. Represent the word problem in an equation.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract fractions and mixed numbers with different denominators by using related fractions, estimate answers, and solve one- and two-step word problems using models and equations.

subject: Math
description:
Classify quadrilaterals into categories based on their properties.
  • Explain that attributes belonging to a category of quadrilaterals also belong to all subcategories of that category.
  • Classify quadrilaterals in a hierarchy based on properties.
description_plain: Your child can sort four-sided shapes into groups based on their features and explain how shapes in bigger groups share features with shapes in smaller groups.

subject: Math
description: Graph points in the first quadrant of a coordinate plane, and identify and interpret the x and y coordinates to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child can plot points on a graph in the first quadrant, read their x and y coordinates, and use them to solve problems.

subject: Math
description:
Relate volume to the operations of multiplication and addition.
  • Find the volume of a rectangular prism with whole-number side lengths by packing it with unit cubes, and show that the volume is the same as would be found by multiplying the edge lengths.
  • Build understanding of the volume formula for rectangular prisms with whole-number edge lengths in the context of solving problems.
  • Find volume of solid figures with one-digit dimensions composed of two non-overlapping rectangular prisms.
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of rectangular boxes by counting cubes or multiplying side lengths, understand the volume formula, and find volume of shapes made from two boxes.

subject: Math
description: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and measure volume by counting unit cubes, using cubic centimeters, cubic inches, cubic feet, and improvised units.
description_plain: Your child understands volume as how much space a solid takes up and can measure it by counting cubes using different units like cubic centimeters or inches.

subject: Math
description: Given a conversion chart, use multiplicative reasoning to solve one-step conversion problems within a given measurement system.
description_plain: Your child can use a conversion chart and multiplication to solve simple measurement conversion problems.

subject: Math
description:
Represent and interpret data.
  • Collect data by asking a question that yields data that changes over time.
  • Make and interpret a representation of data using a line graph.
  • Determine whether a survey question will yield categorical or numerical data, or data that changes over time.
description_plain: Your child can collect data by asking questions that change over time, make and read line graphs, and decide if survey questions give categories, numbers, or changing data.

subject: Math
description:
Compute and solve real-world problems with multi-digit whole numbers and decimal numbers.
  • Add and subtract decimals to thousandths using models, drawings or strategies based on place value.
  • Multiply decimals with a product to thousandths using models, drawings, or strategies based on place value.
  • Divide a whole number by a decimal and divide a decimal by a whole number, using repeated subtraction or area models.
Decimals should be limited to hundredths.
  • Use estimation strategies to assess reasonableness of answers.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals (up to hundredths) using models and place value strategies, solve real-world problems, and check if answers make sense by estimating.

subject: Math
description: Find quotients with remainders when dividing whole numbers with up to four-digit dividends and two-digit divisors using rectangular arrays, area models, repeated subtraction, partial quotients, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division. Use models to make connections and develop the algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can divide whole numbers with up to four digits by two-digit numbers using models, repeated subtraction, and other strategies, and understand how division and multiplication relate.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate fluency with the multiplication of two whole numbers up to a three-digit number by a two-digit number using the standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can multiply two whole numbers, where one number has up to three digits and the other up to two digits, using the standard multiplication method.

subject: Math
description:
Read, write, and compare decimals to thousandths.
  • Write decimals using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form.
  • Compare two decimals to thousandths based on the value of the digits in each place, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and compare decimals to the thousandths place using numbers, words, expanded form, and symbols like >, =, and <.

subject: Math
description:
Explain the patterns in the place value system from one million to the thousandths place.
  • Explain that in a multi-digit number, a digit in one place represents 10 times as much as it represents in the place to its right and 1/10 of what it represents in the place to its left.
  • Explain patterns in products and quotients when numbers are multiplied by 1,000, 100, 10, 0.1, and 0.01 and/or divided by 10 and 100.
description_plain: Your child can explain how each digit in a number is 10 times bigger than the digit to its right and one-tenth the size of the digit to its left, and can describe what happens when numbers are multiplied or divided by 10, 100, or 1,000.

subject: Math
description:
Generate two numerical patterns using two given rules.
  • Identify apparent relationships between corresponding terms.
  • Form ordered pairs consisting of corresponding terms from the two patterns.
  • Graph the ordered pairs on a coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can create two number patterns from given rules, find how the numbers in the patterns relate to each other, pair matching numbers from each pattern, and plot these pairs on a graph.

subject: Math
description:
Write, explain, and evaluate numerical expressions involving the four operations to solve up to two-step problems. Include expressions involving:
  • Parentheses, using the order of operations.
  • Commutative, associative and distributive properties.
description_plain: Your child can write and solve math problems that use addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division in two steps, including using parentheses and understanding properties like changing the order or grouping of numbers.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Compose larger shapes from simple shapes.
description_plain: Your child can put together simple shapes to make bigger shapes.

subject: Math
description:
Model shapes in the world by:
  • Building and drawing triangles, rectangles, squares, hexagons, circles.
  • Building cubes, cones, spheres, and cylinders.
description_plain: Your child can build and draw basic flat shapes like triangles and circles, and build 3D shapes like cubes and cones.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and compare two- and three-dimensional shapes, in different sizes and orientations, using informal language to describe their similarities, differences, attributes and other properties.
description_plain: Your child can look at flat and solid shapes of different sizes and positions and talk about how they are alike and different using simple words.

subject: Math
description: Identify squares, circles, triangles, rectangles, hexagons, cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres as two-dimensional or three-dimensional.
description_plain: Your child can tell which shapes are flat (like squares and circles) and which are solid (like cubes and spheres).

subject: Math
description: Correctly name squares, circles, triangles, rectangles, hexagons, cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres regardless of their orientations or overall size.
description_plain: Your child can correctly name shapes like squares, circles, triangles, and solid shapes no matter how they are turned or how big they are.

subject: Math
description: Describe objects in the environment using names of shapes, and describe the relative positions of objects using positional terms.
description_plain: Your child can use shape names to describe things around them and use words to explain where things are located.

subject: Math
description: Classify objects into given categories; count the numbers of objects in each category and sort the categories by count.
description_plain: Your child can sort objects into groups, count how many are in each group, and put the groups in order from smallest to largest or vice versa.

subject: Math
description: Directly compare two objects with a measurable attribute in common, to see which object has "more of"/"less of" the attribute, and describe the difference.
description_plain: Your child can compare two objects to see which one is bigger or smaller in size, weight, or length and explain the difference.

subject: Math
description: Describe measurable attributes of objects; and describe several different measurable attributes of a single object.
description_plain: Your child can describe things you can measure about objects, like length, weight, or size, and talk about several of these measurements for one object.

subject: Math
description:
Compose and decompose numbers from 11 to 19 into ten ones and some further ones by:
  • Using objects or drawings.
  • Recording each composition or decomposition by a drawing or expression.
  • Understanding that these numbers are composed of ten ones and one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine ones.
description_plain: Your child can break apart numbers from 11 to 19 into ten ones and some more ones using objects or drawings and can show this with pictures or number sentences.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate fluency with addition and subtraction within 5.
description_plain: Your child can quickly and accurately add and subtract numbers up to 5.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and combine groups with totals up to 5 (conceptual subitizing).
description_plain: Your child can recognize and combine small groups of objects that add up to 5 without counting each one.

subject: Math
description: For any number from 0 to 10, find the number that makes 10 when added to the given number using objects or drawings, and record the answer with a drawing or expression.
description_plain: Your child can find the number that, when added to a given number from 0 to 10, makes 10, using objects or drawings and can show the answer with pictures or number sentences.

subject: Math
description: Decompose numbers less than or equal to 10 into pairs in more than one way using objects or drawings, and record each decomposition by a drawing or expression.
description_plain: Your child can break apart numbers up to 10 in different ways using objects or drawings and can show each way with pictures or number sentences.

subject: Math
description:
Solve addition and subtraction word problems, within 10, using objects or drawings to represent the problem, when solving:
  • Add to/Take From-Result Unknown
  • Put Together/Take Apart (Total Unknown and Two Addends Unknown)
description_plain: Your child can solve simple addition and subtraction story problems within 10 by using objects or drawings to understand the problem.

subject: Math
description:
Represent addition and subtraction, within 10:
  • Use a variety of representations such as objects, fingers, mental images, drawings, sounds, acting out situations, verbal explanations, or expressions.
  • Demonstrate understanding of addition and subtraction by making connections among representations.
description_plain: Your child can show addition and subtraction within 10 using things like objects, fingers, drawings, or acting out, and can explain how these ways are connected.

subject: Math
description: Compare two numbers, within 10, presented as written numerals.
description_plain: Your child can compare two written numbers up to 10 and tell which is bigger, smaller, or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description: Identify whether the number of objects, within 10, in one group is greater than, less than, or equal to the number of objects in another group, by using matching and counting strategies.
description_plain: Your child can look at two groups of up to 10 objects and decide which group has more, less, or if they are the same by matching or counting.

subject: Math
description:
Count to answer "How many?" in the following situations:
  • Given a number from 1–20, count out that many objects.
  • Given up to 20 objects, name the next successive number when an object is added, recognizing the quantity is one more/greater.
  • Given 20 objects arranged in a line, a rectangular array, and a circle, identify how many.
  • Given 10 objects in a scattered arrangement, identify how many.
description_plain: Your child can count out a specific number of objects up to 20, say the next number when one more object is added, and count objects arranged in different ways.

subject: Math
description:
Understand the relationship between numbers and quantities.
  • When counting objects, say the number names in the standard order, pairing each object with one and only one number name and each number name with one and only one object (one-to-one correspondence).
  • Recognize that the last number named tells the number of objects counted regardless of their arrangement (cardinality).
  • State the number of objects in a group, of up to 5 objects, without counting the objects (perceptual subitizing).
description_plain: Your child understands that when counting objects, each object matches one number name, the last number said tells how many objects there are, and can recognize small groups up to 5 without counting.

subject: Math
description: Write numbers from 0 to 20. Represent a number of objects with a written numeral 0-20, with 0 representing a count of no objects.
description_plain: Your child can write numbers from 0 to 20 and match written numbers to groups of objects, with 0 meaning no objects.

subject: Math
description: Count forward beginning from a given number within the known sequence, instead of having to begin at 1.
description_plain: Your child can count forward starting from any number within the numbers they know, not just from 1.

subject: Math
description:
Know number names and recognize patterns in the counting sequence by:
  • Counting to 100 by ones.
  • Counting to 100 by tens.
description_plain: Your child knows number names and can count to 100 by ones and by tens.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Reason with two-dimensional shapes and their attributes. • Investigate, describe, and reason about composing triangles and quadrilaterals and decomposing quadrilaterals. • Recognize and draw examples and non-examples of types of quadrilaterals including rhombuses, rectangles, squares, parallelograms, and trapezoids.
description_plain: Your child can explore and describe flat shapes like triangles and four-sided shapes, understand how some shapes can be made by putting others together or taken apart, and can draw examples and non-examples of shapes like rectangles and trapezoids.

subject: Math
description: Compare two fractions with the same numerator or the same denominator by reasoning about their size, using area and length models, and using the >, <, and = symbols. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two fractions refer to the same whole with denominators: halves, fourths and eighths; thirds and sixths.
description_plain: Your child can compare two fractions that have the same top number or the same bottom number by thinking about their size using pictures or models and use >, <, or = to show which is bigger or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description:
Represent equivalent fractions with area and length models by:
  • Composing and decomposing fractions into equivalent fractions using related fractions: halves, fourths and eighths; thirds and sixths.
  • Explaining that a fraction with the same numerator and denominator equals one whole.
  • Expressing whole numbers as fractions, and recognize fractions that are equivalent to whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can show fractions that are equal using pictures, break fractions into smaller equal parts, explain that a fraction with the same top and bottom number equals one whole, and write whole numbers as fractions.

subject: Math
description:
Interpret fractions with denominators of 2, 3, 4, 6, and 8 using area and length models.
  • Using an area model, explain that the numerator of a fraction represents the number of equal parts of the unit fraction.
  • Using a number line, explain that the numerator of a fraction represents the number of lengths of the unit fraction from 0.
description_plain: Your child can understand fractions with denominators 2, 3, 4, 6, and 8 by using pictures and number lines to explain what the top number means.

subject: Math
description:
Interpret unit fractions with denominators of 2, 3, 4, 6, and 8 as quantities formed when a whole is partitioned into equal parts;
  • Explain that a unit fraction is one of those parts.
  • Represent and identify unit fractions using area and length models.
description_plain: Your child understands that a unit fraction is one equal part of a whole divided into 2, 3, 4, 6, or 8 parts and can show and name these fractions using pictures and models.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving perimeters of polygons, including finding the perimeter given the side lengths, and finding an unknown side length.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about the perimeter of shapes by adding side lengths or finding a missing side length.

subject: Math
description:
Relate area to the operations of multiplication and addition.
  • Find the area of a rectangle with whole-number side lengths by tiling it, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths.
  • Multiply side lengths to find areas of rectangles with whole-number side lengths in the context of solving problems, and represent whole-number products as rectangular areas in mathematical reasoning.
  • Use tiles and/or arrays to illustrate and explain that the area of a rectangle can be found by partitioning it into two smaller rectangles, and that the area of the large rectangle is the sum of the two smaller rectangles.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of rectangles by counting squares or multiplying side lengths, and can explain that the area of a big rectangle is the sum of areas of smaller rectangles inside it.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of a rectangle with whole-number side lengths by tiling without gaps or overlaps and counting unit squares.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of a rectangle by covering it with squares without gaps or overlaps and counting the squares.

subject: Math
description:
Solve problems involving customary measurement.
  • Estimate and measure lengths in customary units to the quarter-inch and half-inch, and feet and yards to the whole unit.
  • Estimate and measure capacity and weight in customary units to a whole number: cups, pints, quarts, gallons, ounces, and pounds.
  • Add, subtract, multiply, or divide to solve one-step word problems involving whole number measurements of length, weight, and capacity in the same customary units.
description_plain: Your child can estimate and measure length, weight, and capacity using customary units like inches, feet, pounds, and cups, and can solve simple word problems using these measurements.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time to the nearest minute. Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of time intervals within the same hour.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the nearest minute and solve word problems about adding and subtracting time within the same hour.

subject: Math
description:
Represent and interpret scaled picture and bar graphs:
  • Collect data by asking a question that yields data in up to four categories.
  • Make a representation of data and interpret data in a frequency table, scaled picture graph, and/or scaled bar graph with axes provided.
  • Solve one and two-step "how many more" and "how many less" problems using information from these graphs.
description_plain: Your child can collect data by asking questions, make and read simple graphs like bar and picture graphs, and solve problems about how many more or less items there are.

subject: Math
description: Use concrete and pictorial models, based on place value and the properties of operations, to find the product of a one-digit whole number by a multiple of 10 in the range 10–90.
description_plain: Your child can use models and pictures to multiply a one-digit number by multiples of 10 from 10 to 90.

subject: Math
description:
Add and subtract whole numbers up to and including 1,000.
  • Use estimation strategies to assess reasonableness of answers.
  • Model and explain how the relationship between addition and subtraction can be applied to solve addition and subtraction problems.
  • Use expanded form to decompose numbers and then find sums and differences.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 1,000, check if answers make sense, explain how addition and subtraction are related, and break numbers apart to help with adding and subtracting.

subject: Math
description: Interpret patterns of multiplication on a hundreds board and/or multiplication table.
description_plain: Your child can understand multiplication patterns by looking at a hundreds chart or multiplication table.

subject: Math
description: Solve two-step word problems using addition, subtraction, and multiplication, representing problems using equations with a symbol for the unknown number.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that need two steps using addition, subtraction, and multiplication, and write equations with a symbol for the missing number.

subject: Math
description:
Demonstrate fluency with multiplication and division with factors, quotients and divisors up to and including 10.
  • Know from memory all products with factors up to and including 10.
  • Illustrate and explain using the relationship between multiplication and division.
  • Determine the unknown whole number in a multiplication or division equation relating three whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child knows multiplication and division facts up to 10 from memory, explains how multiplication and division are related, and finds missing numbers in multiplication or division equations.

subject: Math
description: Solve an unknown-factor problem, by using division strategies and/or changing it to a multiplication problem.
description_plain: Your child can find a missing factor in multiplication problems by using division or changing the problem to multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Interpret a multiplication equation as a comparison. Multiply or divide to solve word problems involving multiplicative comparisons using models and equations with a symbol for the unknown number. Distinguish multiplicative comparison from additive comparison.
description_plain: Your child understands multiplication as comparing two amounts, and can solve word problems using multiplication or division with a symbol for the unknown number.

subject: Math
description:
Represent, interpret, and solve one-step problems involving multiplication and division.
  • Solve multiplication word problems with factors up to and including 10. Represent the problem using arrays, pictures, and/or equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
  • Solve division word problems with a divisor and quotient up to and including 10. Represent the problem using arrays, pictures, repeated subtraction and/or equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve one-step multiplication and division word problems with numbers up to 10, using pictures, arrays, or equations with a symbol for the unknown number.

subject: Math
description:
For whole-number quotients of whole numbers with a one-digit divisor and a one-digit quotient:
  • Interpret the divisor and quotient in a division equation as representing the number of equal groups and the number of objects in each group.
  • Illustrate and explain strategies including arrays, repeated addition or subtraction, and decomposing a factor.
description_plain: Your child understands division as making equal groups and can explain division strategies using pictures, repeated addition or subtraction, and breaking apart numbers.

subject: Math
description:
For products of whole numbers with two factors up to and including 10:
  • Interpret the factors as representing the number of equal groups and the number of objects in each group.
  • Illustrate and explain strategies including arrays, repeated addition, decomposing a factor, and applying the commutative and associative properties.
description_plain: Your child understands multiplication as groups of equal size and can explain strategies like arrays and repeated addition to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description:
Partition circles and rectangles into two and four equal shares.
  • Describe the shares as halves and fourths, as half of and fourth of.
  • Describe the whole as two of, or four of the shares.
  • Explain that decomposing into more equal shares creates smaller shares.
description_plain: Your child can divide circles and rectangles into two or four equal parts, call them halves and fourths, and explain that more parts mean smaller pieces.

subject: Math
description:
Create composite shapes by:
  • Making a two-dimensional composite shape using rectangles, squares, trapezoids, triangles, and half-circles naming the components of the new shape.
  • Making a three-dimensional composite shape using cubes, rectangular prisms, cones, and cylinders, naming the components of the new shape.
description_plain: Your child can make new shapes by putting together flat shapes like squares and triangles or 3D shapes like cubes and cones, and name the parts used.

subject: Math
description:
Distinguish between defining and non-defining attributes and create shapes with defining attributes by:
  • Building and drawing triangles, rectangles, squares, trapezoids, hexagons, circles.
  • Building cubes, rectangular prisms, cones, spheres, and cylinders.
description_plain: Your child can tell what makes a shape special and build or draw shapes like triangles, squares, cubes, and cylinders.

subject: Math
description:
Organize, represent, and interpret data with up to three categories.
  • Ask and answer questions about the total number of data points.
  • Ask and answer questions about how many in each category.
  • Ask and answer questions about how many more or less are in one category than in another.
description_plain: Your child can organize and understand data with up to three groups, ask and answer questions about totals and differences between groups.

subject: Math
description: Identify quarters, dimes, and nickels and relate their values to pennies.
description_plain: Your child can identify quarters, dimes, and nickels and understand how much they are worth compared to pennies.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time in hours and half-hours using analog and digital clocks.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the hour and half-hour using both analog and digital clocks.

subject: Math
description:
Measure lengths with non-standard units.
  • Express the length of an object as a whole number of non-standard length units.
  • Measure by laying multiple copies of a shorter object (the length unit) end to end (iterating) with no gaps or overlaps.
description_plain: Your child can measure how long things are by using objects laid end to end without gaps or overlaps and say how many of those objects long something is.

subject: Math
description: Order three objects by length; compare the lengths of two objects indirectly by using a third object.
description_plain: Your child can put three objects in order from shortest to longest and compare two objects by using a third object.

subject: Math
description:
Subtract multiples of 10 in the range 10-90 from multiples of 10 in the range 10-90, explaining the reasoning, using:
  • Concrete models and drawings
  • Number lines
  • Strategies based on place value
  • Properties of operations
  • The relationship between addition and subtraction
description_plain: Your child can subtract multiples of 10 from each other up to 90 and explain how they did it using drawings, number lines, and place value ideas.

subject: Math
description: Given a two-digit number, mentally find 10 more or 10 less than the number, without having to count; explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can quickly find 10 more or 10 less than any two-digit number without counting and explain how they know.

subject: Math
description:
Using concrete models or drawings, strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and explaining the reasoning used, add, within 100, in the following situations:
  • A two-digit number and a one-digit number
  • A two-digit number and a multiple of 10
description_plain: Your child can add numbers within 100 using place value and drawings, adding a two-digit number to a one-digit number or to a multiple of 10.

subject: Math
description: Compare two two-digit numbers based on the value of the tens and ones digits, recording the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, and <.
description_plain: Your child can compare two two-digit numbers by looking at their tens and ones and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description:
Understand that the two digits of a two-digit number represent amounts of tens and ones.
  • Unitize by making a ten from a collection of ten ones.
  • Model the numbers from 11 to 19 as composed of a ten and one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine ones.
  • Demonstrate that the numbers 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine tens, with 0 ones.
description_plain: Your child understands that two-digit numbers show how many tens and ones there are, can make a ten from ten ones, and knows numbers like 10, 20, 30 mean tens with zero ones.

subject: Math
description: Read and write numerals, and represent a number of objects with a written numeral, to 100.
description_plain: Your child can read and write numbers up to 100 and match numbers to groups of objects.

subject: Math
description: Count to 150, starting at any number less than 150.
description_plain: Your child can count up to 150, starting from any number less than 150.

subject: Math
description: Determine the unknown whole number in an addition or subtraction equation involving three whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can find the missing number in addition or subtraction problems with three numbers.

subject: Math
description: Apply understanding of the equal sign to determine if equations involving addition and subtraction are true.
description_plain: Your child understands the equal sign and can tell if addition or subtraction equations are true.

subject: Math
description:
Add and subtract, within 20, using strategies such as:
  • Counting on
  • Making ten
  • Decomposing a number leading to a ten
  • Using the relationship between addition and subtraction
  • Using a number line
  • Creating equivalent but simpler or known sums
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers within 20 using different strategies like counting on, making ten, using number lines, and understanding how addition and subtraction relate.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate fluency with addition and subtraction within 10.
description_plain: Your child can quickly and accurately add and subtract numbers within 10.

subject: Math
description: Solve an unknown-addend problem, within 20, by using addition strategies and/or changing it to a subtraction problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems where a number is missing in addition within 20 by using addition or changing the problem to subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Apply the commutative and associative properties as strategies for solving addition problems.
description_plain: Your child uses the ideas that numbers can be added in any order or grouped in different ways to help solve addition problems.

subject: Math
description: Represent and solve word problems that call for addition of three whole numbers whose sum is less than or equal to 20, by using objects, drawings, and equations with a symbol for the unknown number.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that add three numbers up to 20 using objects, drawings, or equations with a symbol for the missing number.

subject: Math
description:
Represent and solve addition and subtraction word problems, within 20, with unknowns, by using objects, drawings, and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem, when solving:
  • Add to/Take from-Change Unknown
  • Put together/Take Apart-Addend Unknown
  • Compare-Difference Unknown
description_plain: Your child can solve addition and subtraction word problems within 20 by using objects, drawings, or equations with a symbol for the unknown number.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Compare two decimals to hundredths by reasoning about their size using area and length models, and recording the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, or <. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two decimals refer to the same whole.
description_plain: Your child can compare two decimals to hundredths using models and write >, =, or < to show which is bigger, knowing the decimals must be parts of the same whole.

subject: Math
description:
Use decimal notation to represent fractions.
  • Express, model and explain the equivalence between fractions with denominators of 10 and 100.
  • Use equivalent fractions to add two fractions with denominators of 10 or 100.
  • Represent tenths and hundredths with models, making connections between fractions and decimals.
description_plain: Your child can write fractions as decimals, explain how fractions with 10 or 100 as denominators are equal to decimals, and add fractions with these denominators.

subject: Math
description:
Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to:
  • Model and explain how fractions can be represented by multiplying a whole number by a unit fraction, using this understanding to multiply a whole number by any fraction less than one.
  • Solve word problems involving multiplication of a fraction by a whole number.
description_plain: Your child can show how multiplying a whole number by a fraction less than one works and solve word problems using this idea.

subject: Math
description:
Understand and justify decompositions of fractions with denominators of 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 100.
  • Understand addition and subtraction of fractions as joining and separating parts referring to the same whole.
  • Decompose a fraction into a sum of unit fractions and a sum of fractions with the same denominator in more than one way using area models, length models, and equations.
  • Add and subtract fractions, including mixed numbers with like denominators, by replacing each mixed number with an equivalent fraction, and/or by using properties of operations and the relationship between addition and subtraction.
  • Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions, including mixed numbers by writing equations from a visual representation of the problem.
description_plain: Your child understands how to add and subtract fractions with the same denominator, can break fractions into smaller parts in different ways, and solve word problems with fractions.

subject: Math
description:
Compare two fractions with different numerators and different denominators, using the denominators 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 100. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two fractions refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with symbols >, =, or <, and justify the conclusions by:
  • Reasoning about their size and using area and length models.
  • Using benchmark fractions 0, ½, and a whole.
  • Comparing common numerator or common denominators.
description_plain: Your child can compare fractions with different numerators and denominators by thinking about their size, using models, and writing >, =, or <, making sure the fractions are parts of the same whole.

subject: Math
description: Explain why a fraction is equivalent to another fraction by using area and length fraction models, with attention to how the number and size of the parts differ even though the two fractions themselves are the same size.
description_plain: Your child can explain why two fractions are equal by using models that show how the parts look different but are the same size.

subject: Math
description: Recognize symmetry in a two-dimensional figure, and identify and draw lines of symmetry.
description_plain: Your child can find and draw lines of symmetry in flat shapes.

subject: Math
description: Classify quadrilaterals and triangles based on angle measure, side lengths, and the presence or absence of parallel or perpendicular lines.
description_plain: Your child can sort four-sided shapes and triangles by their angles, side lengths, and whether they have parallel or perpendicular sides.

subject: Math
description: Draw and identify points, lines, line segments, rays, angles, and perpendicular and parallel lines.
description_plain: Your child can draw and name points, lines, line segments, rays, angles, and identify perpendicular and parallel lines.

subject: Math
description:
Develop an understanding of angles and angle measurement.
  • Understand angles as geometric shapes that are formed wherever two rays share a common endpoint, and are measured in degrees.
  • Measure and sketch angles in whole-number degrees using a protractor.
  • Solve addition and subtraction problems to find unknown angles on a diagram in real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child understands angles as shapes made by two rays with a common endpoint, can measure and draw angles using a protractor, and solve problems to find missing angles.

subject: Math
description:
Solve problems with area and perimeter.
  • Find areas of rectilinear figures with known side lengths.
  • Solve problems involving a fixed area and varying perimeters and a fixed perimeter and varying areas.
  • Apply the area and perimeter formulas for rectangles in real world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the area and perimeter of shapes with straight sides and solve problems where the area or perimeter changes, using formulas for rectangles in real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of time intervals that cross the hour.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that involve adding and subtracting time intervals that go past the hour.

subject: Math
description: Use multiplicative reasoning to convert metric measurements from a larger unit to a smaller unit using place value understanding, two-column tables, and length models.
description_plain: Your child can change metric measurements from bigger units to smaller units by using place value, tables, and length models.

subject: Math
description:
Know relative sizes of measurement units. Solve problems involving metric measurement.
  • Measure to solve problems involving metric units: centimeter, meter, gram, kilogram, Liter, milliliter.
  • Add, subtract, multiply, and divide to solve one-step word problems involving whole-number measurements of length, mass, and capacity that are given in metric units.
description_plain: Your child understands sizes of metric units like centimeters, meters, grams, kilograms, liters, and milliliters, and can solve one-step word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with these units.

subject: Math
description:
Represent and interpret data using whole numbers.
  • Collect data by asking a question that yields numerical data.
  • Make a representation of data and interpret data in a frequency table, scaled bar graph, and/or line plot.
  • Determine whether a survey question will yield categorical or numerical data.
description_plain: Your child can collect number data by asking questions, make charts or graphs like bar graphs and line plots, and tell if a survey question will give number data or categories.

subject: Math
description: Find whole-number quotients and remainders with up to three-digit dividends and one-digit divisors with place value understanding using rectangular arrays, area models, repeated subtraction, partial quotients, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division.
description_plain: Your child can divide numbers up to three digits by one-digit numbers and find the quotient and remainder using different methods like arrays, area models, or repeated subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Multiply a whole number of up to three digits by a one-digit whole number, and multiply up to two two-digit numbers with place value understanding using area models, partial products, and the properties of operations. Use models to make connections and develop the algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can multiply a number up to three digits by a one-digit number and multiply two numbers up to two digits using place value and models to understand the process.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract multi-digit whole numbers up to and including 100,000 using the standard algorithm with place value understanding.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract whole numbers up to 100,000 using the standard method and understanding place value.

subject: Math
description: Compare two multi-digit numbers up to and including 100,000 based on the values of the digits in each place, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can compare two numbers up to 100,000 by looking at the value of each digit and use >, =, or < symbols to show which is bigger or smaller.

subject: Math
description: Read and write multi-digit whole numbers up to and including 100,000 using numerals, number names, and expanded form.
description_plain: Your child can read and write numbers up to 100,000 using digits, words, and expanded form.

subject: Math
description: Explain that in a multi-digit whole number, a digit in one place represents 10 times as much as it represents in the place to its right, up to 100,000.
description_plain: Your child understands that in a multi-digit number, each digit is worth 10 times more than the digit to its right, up to 100,000.

subject: Math
description: Generate and analyze a number or shape pattern that follows a given rule.
description_plain: Your child can create and study number or shape patterns that follow a specific rule.

subject: Math
description:
Find all factor pairs for whole numbers up to and including 50 to:
  • Recognize that a whole number is a multiple of each of its factors.
  • Determine whether a given whole number is a multiple of a given one-digit number.
  • Determine if the number is prime or composite.
description_plain: Your child can find all factor pairs of numbers up to 50, know that numbers are multiples of their factors, decide if a number is a multiple of a one-digit number, and tell if a number is prime or composite.

subject: Math
description:
Solve two-step word problems involving the four operations with whole numbers.
  • Use estimation strategies to assess reasonableness of answers.
  • Interpret remainders in word problems.
  • Represent problems using equations with a letter standing for the unknown quantity.
description_plain: Your child can solve two-step word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division, estimate answers to check if they make sense, understand remainders, and write equations with letters for unknowns.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description:
Qualitatively analyze the functional relationship between two quantities.
  • Analyze a graph determining where the function is increasing or decreasing; linear or non-linear.
  • Sketch a graph that exhibits the qualitative features of a real-world function.
description_plain: Your child can look at graphs to see where a function goes up or down and whether it is a straight line or curved, and draw graphs that show these features for real-life situations.

subject: Math
description:
Analyze functions that model linear relationships.
  • Understand that a linear relationship can be generalized by y = mx + b.
  • Write an equation in slope-intercept form to model a linear relationship by determining the rate of change and the initial value, given at least two (x, y) values or a graph.
  • Construct a graph of a linear relationship given an equation in slope-intercept form.
  • Interpret the rate of change and initial value of a linear function in terms of the situation it models, and in terms of the slope and y-intercept of its graph or a table of values.
description_plain: Your child can understand and write equations for straight-line relationships using y = mx + b, make graphs from these equations, and explain what the slope and starting point mean in real situations.

subject: Math
description: Identify linear functions from tables, equations, and graphs.
description_plain: Your child can identify straight-line functions when given tables, equations, or graphs.

subject: Math
description: Compare properties of two linear functions each represented in a different way (algebraically, graphically, numerically in tables, or by verbal descriptions).
description_plain: Your child can compare two straight-line functions shown in different ways, like equations, graphs, tables, or words.

subject: Math
description:
Understand that a function is a rule that assigns to each input exactly one output.
  • Recognize functions when graphed as the set of ordered pairs consisting of an input and exactly one corresponding output.
  • Recognize functions given a table of values or a set of ordered pairs.
description_plain: Your child understands that a function is a rule that gives exactly one output for each input and can recognize functions from graphs, tables, or lists of pairs.

subject: Math
description:
Understand that patterns of association can also be seen in bivariate categorical data by displaying frequencies and relative frequencies in a two-way table.
  • Construct and interpret a two-way table summarizing data on two categorical variables collected from the same subjects.
  • Use relative frequencies calculated for rows or columns to describe possible association between the two variables.
description_plain: Your child can make and read two-way tables that show how two categories relate and use percentages to describe possible connections between them.

subject: Math
description: Use the equation of a linear model to solve problems in the context of bivariate quantitative data, interpreting the slope and y-intercept.
description_plain: Your child can use an equation for a straight line to solve problems with two related quantities and explain what the slope and starting value mean.

subject: Math
description:
Model the relationship between bivariate quantitative data to:
  • Informally fit a straight line for a scatter plot that suggests a linear association.
  • Informally assess the model fit by judging the closeness of the data points to the line.
description_plain: Your child can draw a straight line that fits a scatter plot showing two related quantities and judge how well the line matches the data points.

subject: Math
description: Construct and interpret scatter plots for bivariate measurement data to investigate patterns of association between two quantities. Investigate and describe patterns such as clustering, outliers, positive or negative association, linear association, and nonlinear association.
description_plain: Your child can make and understand scatter plots to find patterns like groups, outliers, and whether the relationship is positive, negative, straight, or curved.

subject: Math
description:
Analyze and solve a system of two linear equations in two variables in slope-intercept form.
  • Understand that solutions to a system of two linear equations correspond to the points of intersection of their graphs because the point of intersection satisfies both equations simultaneously.
  • Solve real-world and mathematical problems leading to systems of linear equations by graphing the equations. Solve simple cases by inspection.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems with two linear equations by finding where their graphs cross and understand that this point solves both equations at the same time.

subject: Math
description:
Solve real-world and mathematical problems by writing and solving equations and inequalities in one variable.
  • Recognize linear equations in one variable as having one solution, infinitely many solutions, or no solutions.
  • Solve linear equations and inequalities including multi-step equations and inequalities with the same variable on both sides.
description_plain: Your child can write and solve equations and inequalities with one variable, including those with multiple steps and variables on both sides, and know when there is one solution, many solutions, or none.

subject: Math
description: Perform multiplication and division with numbers expressed in scientific notation to solve real-world problems, including problems where both decimal and scientific notation are used.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide numbers written in scientific notation to solve real-world problems, even when mixing decimal and scientific forms.

subject: Math
description: Use numbers expressed in scientific notation to estimate very large or very small quantities and to express how many times as much one is than the other.
description_plain: Your child can use scientific notation to estimate very big or very small numbers and compare how many times bigger one is than another.

subject: Math
description:
Use square root and cube root symbols to:
  • Represent solutions to equations of the form x² = p and x³ =p, where p is a positive rational number.
  • Evaluate square roots of perfect squares and cube roots of perfect cubes for positive numbers less than or equal to 400.
description_plain: Your child can use square root and cube root symbols to solve equations like x² = p or x³ = p, and find roots of perfect squares and cubes up to 400.

subject: Math
description: Develop and apply the properties of integer exponents to generate equivalent numerical expressions.
description_plain: Your child can use rules about exponents to rewrite expressions in different but equal ways.

subject: Math
description:
Use rational approximations of irrational numbers to compare the size of irrational numbers and locate them approximately on a number line. Estimate the value of expressions involving:
  • Square roots and cube roots to the tenths.
  • π to the hundredths.
description_plain: Your child can estimate and compare irrational numbers by using close rational numbers and place them approximately on a number line, including estimating roots and π.

subject: Math
description: Understand that every number has a decimal expansion. Building upon the definition of a rational number, know that an irrational number is defined as a non-repeating, non-terminating decimal.
description_plain: Your child understands that every number can be written as a decimal, and that irrational numbers are decimals that never repeat or end.

subject: Math
description: Understand how the formulas for the volumes of cones, cylinders, and spheres are related and use the relationship to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child understands how the formulas for the volumes of cones, cylinders, and spheres are connected and can use this to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points in a coordinate system.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points on a coordinate grid.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem and its converse to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem and its reverse to solve real-world and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Explain the Pythagorean Theorem and its converse.
description_plain: Your child can explain what the Pythagorean Theorem says and understand its reverse.

subject: Math
description:
Use informal arguments to analyze angle relationships.
  • Recognize relationships between interior and exterior angles of a triangle.
  • Recognize the relationships between the angles created when parallel lines are cut by a transversal.
  • Recognize the angle-angle criterion for similarity of triangles.
  • Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving angles.
description_plain: Your child can understand and use angle relationships, like those inside and outside triangles, angles made by parallel lines, and when triangles are similar, to solve problems.

subject: Math
description:
Use transformations to define similarity.
  • Verify experimentally the properties of dilations that create similar figures.
  • Understand that a two-dimensional figure is similar to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of rotations, reflections, translations, and dilations.
  • Given two similar two-dimensional figures, describe a sequence that exhibits the similarity between them.
description_plain: Your child can use transformations like resizing, rotating, flipping, and sliding to show when shapes are similar and describe how one shape changes into another.

subject: Math
description: Describe the effect of dilations about the origin, translations, rotations about the origin in 90 degree increments, and reflections across the x-axis and y-axis on two-dimensional figures using coordinates.
description_plain: Your child can describe how changing a shape by resizing, sliding, rotating, or flipping affects its position using coordinates.

subject: Math
description:
Use transformations to define congruence.
  • Verify experimentally the properties of rotations, reflections, and translations that create congruent figures.
  • Understand that a two-dimensional figure is congruent to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of rotations, reflections, and translations.
  • Given two congruent figures, describe a sequence that exhibits the congruence between them.
description_plain: Your child can use rotations, reflections, and slides to show when two shapes are exactly the same size and shape, and explain how one matches the other.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Design and use a simulation to generate frequencies for compound events.
description_plain: Your child can create and use simulations to find how often combined events happen.

subject: Math
description: For an event described in everyday language, identify the outcomes in the sample space which compose the event, when the sample space is represented using organized lists, tables, and tree diagrams.
description_plain: Your child can find all the possible outcomes that make up an event using lists, tables, or tree diagrams.

subject: Math
description: Understand that, just as with simple events, the probability of a compound event is the fraction of outcomes in the sample space for which the compound event occurs.
description_plain: Your child understands that the chance of a combined event happening is the fraction of all possible outcomes where the event occurs.

subject: Math
description: Determine probabilities of compound events using organized lists, tables, tree diagrams, and simulation.
description_plain: Your child can find the chances of combined events using organized lists, tables, tree diagrams, or simulations.

subject: Math
description: Compare theoretical and experimental probabilities from a model to observed frequencies; if the agreement is not good, explain possible sources of the discrepancy.
description_plain: Your child can compare expected chances with actual results and explain why they might be different.

subject: Math
description: Develop a probability model (which may not be uniform) by repeatedly performing a chance process and observing frequencies in the data generated.
