description_plain: Your child understands that fossils show us the history of Earth and how life has changed.

subject: Science
description: Engage in argument from evidence to explain how differences among animals of the same population sometimes gives individuals an advantage in surviving and reproducing in changing habitats.
description_plain: Your child explains how differences among animals can help some survive and have babies when habitats change.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain that animals receive different types of information through their senses, process the information, and respond to the information in different ways.
description_plain: Your child uses models to show how animals get information through their senses, think about it, and respond in different ways.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain that plants and animals have external structures that function to support survival.
description_plain: Your child uses models to explain how parts outside of plants and animals help them survive.

subject: Science
description: Understand the effects of environmental changes, adaptations, and behaviors that enable organisms to survive in changing habitats.
description_plain: Your child understands how changes in the environment, adaptations, and behaviors help living things survive in new habitats.

subject: Science
description: Carry out investigations to explain how light is refracted and absorbed.
description_plain: Your child does experiments to show how light bends and is absorbed by different materials.

subject: Science
description: Carry out investigations to infer the path light travels from a light source to a mirror and how it is reflected (by the mirror) using different angles.
description_plain: Your child investigates how light travels from a source to a mirror and how it bounces off at different angles.

subject: Science
description: Understand the nature of light and how light interacts with objects.
description_plain: Your child understands what light is and how it interacts with objects.

subject: Science
description: Carry out investigations on common materials to classify them as insulators or conductors of electricity.
description_plain: Your child tests materials to decide if they let electricity pass through or block it.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain a simple electrical circuit and the necessary components.
description_plain: Your child can use simple models to show how an electrical circuit works and what parts it needs.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to identify basic forms of energy (light, sound, heat, and electrical) that cause motion or create change.
description_plain: Your child will ask questions to identify different types of energy like light, sound, heat, and electricity that cause things to move or change.

subject: Science
description: Understand that energy can be transferred from place to place by sound, light, heat, and electric currents.
description_plain: Your child understands that energy can move from one place to another through sound, light, heat, and electric currents.

subject: Science
description: Carry out investigations to explain how electrically charged objects push or pull on other objects to produce motion.
description_plain: Your child can do experiments to show how electrically charged objects can push or pull other objects to make them move.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to summarize the relationship of magnetic interactions between two objects not in contact with each other.
description_plain: Your child will ask questions to explain how magnets can attract or repel objects without touching them.

subject: Science
description: Understand how various forces affect the motion of an object.
description_plain: Your child understands how different forces can change the way an object moves.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain how the sun’s energy drives the processes of the water cycle (including evaporation, transpiration, condensation, precipitation).
description_plain: Your child can use models to explain how the sun’s energy causes water to evaporate, form clouds, and fall as rain or snow.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation to summarize the ocean's influences on weather and climate in North Carolina.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the ocean affects the weather and climate in North Carolina.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret weather data to explain current and upcoming weather conditions (including severe weather such as hurricanes and tornadoes) in a given location.
description_plain: Your child can look at weather data to explain current and upcoming weather, including storms like hurricanes and tornadoes.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to compare daily and seasonal changes in weather conditions (including wind speed and direction, precipitation, and temperature) and patterns.
description_plain: Your child can compare daily and seasonal weather changes like wind, rain, and temperature by studying data.

subject: Science
description: Understand how Earth systems (hydrosphere and atmosphere) impact patterns of weather and climate.
description_plain: Your child understands how Earth’s water and air systems affect weather and climate patterns.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to compare inherited and acquired traits.
description_plain: Your child will ask questions to tell the difference between traits inherited from parents and traits learned during life.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to compare instincts and learned behaviors.
description_plain: Your child will ask questions to compare instincts animals are born with and behaviors they learn.

subject: Science
description: Understand some characteristics of an organism are inherited and other characteristics are acquired.
description_plain: Your child understands that some traits come from parents while others are learned or acquired.

subject: Science
description: Use models to infer the effects that may result from the interconnected relationships of plants and animals to their ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child can use models to show how plants and animals depend on each other in their environment.

subject: Science
description: Use models to classify organisms within an ecosystem according to the function they serve: producers, consumers, or decomposers .
description_plain: Your child can use models to group living things in an ecosystem as producers, consumers, or decomposers based on what they do.

subject: Science
description: Engage in argument from evidence to compare the characteristics of several common ecosystems (including estuaries and salt marshes, oceans, lakes and ponds, rivers and streams, forests, and grasslands) in terms of their ability to support a variety of populations.
description_plain: Your child can compare different ecosystems like forests, oceans, and grasslands to see how well they support plants and animals.

subject: Science
description: Understand the interdependence of plants and animals within their ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child understands how plants and animals rely on each other to live in their ecosystem.

subject: Science
description: Use models to compare the major systems of the human body (digestive, respiratory, circulatory, muscular, skeletal, nervous) as it relates to their functions necessary for life.
description_plain: Your child can use models to compare major human body systems and explain how they help us live.

subject: Science
description: Use models to recognize the organizational structure of humans as a multicellular organism (cell, tissue, organ, system, organism).
description_plain: Your child can use models to show how the human body is organized from cells to tissues, organs, and systems.

subject: Science
description: Understand how structures and systems of the human body perform functions necessary for life.
description_plain: Your child understands how the parts and systems of the human body work together to keep us alive.

subject: Science
description: Use mathematics and computational thinking to infer the motion of an object (including position, direction, and speed).
description_plain: Your child can use math and thinking skills to figure out how an object moves, including its position, direction, and speed.

subject: Science
description: Carry out investigations to explain how factors such as gravity, friction, and change in mass affect the motion of objects.
description_plain: Your child can do experiments to explain how gravity, friction, and weight changes affect how objects move.

subject: Science
description: Understand force, motion, and the relationship between them.
description_plain: Your child understands the connection between forces and motion.

subject: Science
description: Carry out investigations to compare how heating and cooling affect some materials and how this relates to their purpose and practical applications.
description_plain: Your child can investigate how heating and cooling change materials and why those changes matter for how we use them.

subject: Science
description: Carry out investigations to explain whether the mixing of two or more substances results in new substances.
description_plain: Your child can do experiments to find out if mixing substances creates new materials.

subject: Science
description: Carry out investigations to compare the weight of objects before and after an interaction.
description_plain: Your child can compare the weight of objects before and after they interact to see if it changes.

subject: Science
description: Understand the interactions of matter and energy and the changes that occur.
description_plain: Your child understands how matter and energy interact and cause changes.

subject: Science
description: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to compare the implications of sustainable and unsustainable land use practices (including agriculture and deforestation) and the importance of stewardship.
description_plain: Your child can find and share information about how using land in good or bad ways affects the environment and why caring for the land matters.

subject: Science
description: Engage in argument from evidence to explain that the good health of humans and the environment requires: monitoring of the lithosphere, maintaining soil quality and stewardship.
description_plain: Your child can explain why keeping soil healthy and watching the land carefully helps both people and nature stay healthy.

subject: Science
description: Understand the reciprocal relationship between the lithosphere and humans.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and the Earth’s land affect each other.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain the rock cycle and its relationship to the formation of soil (including how different types of soil come from different types of rocks).
description_plain: Your child can use models to explain the rock cycle and how different rocks help form different types of soil.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation to illustrate how the movement of lithospheric plates can create geologic landforms and cause major geologic events such as earthquakes and volcanic eruptions.
description_plain: Your child can explain how moving plates under the Earth’s surface create landforms and cause earthquakes and volcanoes.

subject: Science
description: Use models to summarize the structure of the earth, including the layers, the mantle and core based on the relative position, composition and density.
description_plain: Your child can use models to describe Earth’s layers like the crust, mantle, and core based on their position and materials.

subject: Science
description: Understand the lithosphere and how interactions of constructive and destructive forces have resulted in changes in the surface of the earth over time.
description_plain: Your child understands how forces that build up and break down the Earth’s surface have changed it over time.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data from historical and ongoing space exploration to illustrate the size and scale of the components of our solar system, galaxy, and universe.
description_plain: Your child can study space exploration data to understand the size and scale of the solar system, galaxy, and universe.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain how the gravitational forces of the Sun and planets impact the structure of our solar system.
description_plain: Your child can use models to explain how the Sun’s and planets’ gravity shape our solar system.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to compare the planets in our solar system in terms of: size and gravitational force relative to Earth, surface and atmospheric features, relative distance from the sun, and ability to support life.
description_plain: Your child can compare planets by size, gravity, surface, atmosphere, distance from the sun, and if they can support life.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain how the relative motion and relative position of the Sun, Earth and moon affect the seasons, tides, phases of the moon, and eclipses.
description_plain: Your child can use models to explain how the movements of the Sun, Earth, and moon cause seasons, tides, moon phases, and eclipses.

subject: Science
description: Understand the earth/moon/sun system, and the properties, structures and predictable motions of celestial bodies in the Universe.
description_plain: Your child understands how the Earth, moon, and sun work together and how objects in space move in predictable ways.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to predict how the abiotic factors (such as temperature, water, sunlight, and soil quality) and biotic factors affect the ability of organisms to grow and survive in different biomes (freshwater, marine, temperate forest, rainforest, grassland, desert, taiga, tundra).
description_plain: Your child can look at data to understand how things like temperature, water, sunlight, soil, and living things affect how plants and animals grow and survive in different environments like forests, deserts, and oceans.

subject: Science
description: Use models to summarize how energy derived from the sun is used by plants to produce sugars (photosynthesis) and is transferred to consumers and decomposers.
description_plain: Your child can use simple models to show how plants use sunlight to make food and how that energy moves to animals and decomposers.

subject: Science
description: Understand the flow of energy through ecosystems and the responses of populations to the biotic and abiotic factors in their environment.
description_plain: Your child understands how energy moves through nature and how living things respond to their environment.

subject: Science
description: Use models to summarize structural adaptations, processes, and responses that flowering plants use for defense, survival and reproduction.
description_plain: Your child can use models to explain how flowering plants protect themselves, survive, and reproduce.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation to compare how vascular and nonvascular plants obtain, transport, and use nutrients and water necessary for survival.
description_plain: Your child can explain how plants with and without tubes get and move water and nutrients to stay alive.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain how the processes of photosynthesis, respiration, and transpiration work together to meet the needs of plants.
description_plain: Your child can use models to explain how photosynthesis, breathing, and water movement work together to help plants live.

subject: Science
description: Understand the structures, processes, and behaviors of plants that enable them to survive and reproduce.
description_plain: Your child understands how plants’ parts and actions help them survive and make new plants.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain that various waves (seismic, sound, electromagnetic, including visible light) are reflected, absorbed or transmitted through various materials.
description_plain: Your child can use models to show how different waves like sound, light, and earthquake waves bounce off, pass through, or get absorbed by materials.

subject: Science
description: Carry out investigations to conclude the relationship between sound waves (including rate of vibration, the medium through which vibrations travel) and hearing.
description_plain: Your child can do experiments to understand how sound waves and the way they travel help us hear.

subject: Science
description: Carry out investigations to conclude the relationship between the electromagnetic spectrum (including visible light) and sight.
description_plain: Your child can do experiments to learn how light waves help us see.

subject: Science
description: Use models of a simple wave to explain wave properties in seismic, light, and sound waves that include: waves having a repeating pattern with a specific amplitude, frequency, and wavelength, and the amplitude of a wave is related to the energy of the wave.
description_plain: Your child can use simple wave models to explain wave patterns and how wave size relates to energy in sound, light, and earthquake waves.

subject: Science
description: Understand the properties of waves and the wavelike property of energy in seismic, electromagnetic (including visible light), and sound waves.
description_plain: Your child understands the properties of waves and how energy moves in sound, light, and earthquake waves.

subject: Science
description: Carry out investigations to explain the transfer of electrical energy in electrical circuits, to include how a circuit requires a complete loop through which an electrical current can pass.
description_plain: Your child can do experiments to explain how electricity moves through a complete circuit.

subject: Science
description: Engage in argument from evidence to classify materials as conductors and insulators of energy (both thermal and electrical).
description_plain: Your child can use evidence to decide which materials let heat or electricity pass through and which do not.

subject: Science
description: Carry out investigations to compare the transfer of thermal energy in insulated and non-insulated materials (examples could include insulated box, solar cooker, or styrofoam cup).
description_plain: Your child can do experiments to compare how well insulated and non-insulated materials keep heat.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain how the transfer of heat and resulting change of temperature impacts the behavior of matter to include expansion, and contraction.
description_plain: Your child can use models to explain how heat changes temperature and causes materials to expand or shrink.

subject: Science
description: Use models to compare the directional transfer of heat energy of matter through convection, radiation, and conduction.
description_plain: Your child can use models to compare how heat moves through materials by convection, radiation, and conduction.

subject: Science
description: Understand characteristics of thermal and electrical energy transfer and interactions of matter and energy.
description_plain: Your child understands how heat and electricity move and interact with matter.

subject: Science
description: Carry out investigations to compare the physical properties of pure substances that are independent of the amount of matter present including density, melting point, boiling point and solubility to properties that are dependent on the amount of matter present to include volume, mass and weight.
description_plain: Your child can do experiments to compare properties of pure substances like density and melting point that don’t change with amount, to properties like volume and weight that do.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain the relationship between changes in thermal energy in a substance and the motion of its particles (including phase changes).
description_plain: Your child can use models to explain how adding or removing heat changes how particles move and can change matter from solid to liquid or gas.

subject: Science
description: Use models to illustrate that matter is made of atoms and elements, and are distinguished from each other by the types of atoms that compose them.
description_plain: Your child can use models to show that matter is made of atoms and elements, which are different because of the types of atoms they have.

subject: Science
description: Understand the structure, states, and physical properties of matter.
description_plain: Your child understands what matter is made of, its different states, and its physical properties.

subject: Math
description: Justifying the appropriate choice of measures of center using the shape of the data distribution.
description_plain: Your child can explain why they chose a certain way to find the middle or average of data based on how the data looks.

subject: Math
description: Giving quantitative measures of center, describing variability, and any overall pattern, and noting any striking deviations
description_plain: Your child can find the average, describe how spread out data is, notice overall patterns, and point out anything unusual.

subject: Math
description: Communicating the nature of the attribute under investigation, how it was measured, and the units of measurement.
description_plain: Your child can explain what they are measuring, how they measured it, and what units they used.

subject: Math
description: Reporting the number of observations in dot plots and histograms.
description_plain: Your child can count and report how many data points are shown in dot plots and histograms.

subject: Math
description: Compare the attributes of different representations of the same data.
description_plain: Your child can compare different ways of showing the same data and explain their differences.

subject: Math
description: Use dot plots, histograms, and box plots to represent data.
description_plain: Your child can use dot plots, histograms, and box plots to show data.

subject: Math
description: Understand the median as a measure of center that is the numerical middle of an ordered data set.
description_plain: Your child understands that the median is the middle number when data is put in order.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a mean is a measure of center that represents a balance point or fair share of a data set and can be influenced by the presence of extreme values within the data set.
description_plain: Your child understands that the mean is the average that balances the data and can be affected by very high or low numbers.

subject: Math
description: Using coordinates to find the length of a side joining points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate.
description_plain: Your child can find the length of a side between two points that share the same x or y coordinate on a graph.

subject: Math
description: Drawing polygons in the coordinate plane given coordinates for the vertices.
description_plain: Your child can draw shapes on a graph using the points given.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of special quadrilaterals and polygons by decomposing into triangles or rectangles.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of special four-sided shapes and other polygons by breaking them into triangles or rectangles.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of triangles by composing into rectangles and decomposing into right triangles.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of triangles by making them into rectangles or right triangles.

subject: Math
description: Analyze the relationship between quantities in different representations (context, equations, tables, and graphs).
description_plain: Your child can study how quantities relate to each other using different forms like equations, tables, and graphs.

subject: Math
description: Using variables to represent two quantities in a real-world or mathematical context that change in relationship to one another.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to represent two quantities that change together in real life or math problems.

subject: Math
description: Representing solutions of inequalities on number line diagrams.
description_plain: Your child can show solutions to inequalities on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Recognizing that inequalities of the form x > c or x < c have infinitely many solutions.
description_plain: Your child understands that inequalities like x > c or x < c have many solutions.

subject: Math
description: Writing an inequality of the form x > c or x < c to represent a constraint or condition in a real-world or mathematical problem.
description_plain: Your child can write inequalities like x > c or x < c to describe limits or conditions in problems.

subject: Math
description: Using substitution to determine whether a given number in a specified set makes an inequality true.
description_plain: Your child can check if a number makes an inequality true by substituting it in.

subject: Math
description: p • x = q for cases in which p, q and x are all nonnegative rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can solve multiplication equations like p × x = q when p, q, and x are all nonnegative rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: x + p = q in which p, q and x are all nonnegative rational numbers; and,
description_plain: Your child can solve addition equations like x + p = q when p, q, and x are all nonnegative rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Evaluate expressions at specific values of their variables using expressions that arise from formulas used in real-world problems.
description_plain: Your child can calculate the value of expressions by plugging in numbers, especially for real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: Identify parts of an expression using mathematical terms and view one or more of those parts as a single entity.
description_plain: Your child can identify parts of a math expression and understand that some parts can be grouped together as one.

subject: Math
description: Write expressions that record operations with numbers and with letters standing for numbers.
description_plain: Your child can write math expressions using numbers and letters that stand for numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use models to add and subtract integers from -20 to 20 and describe real-world contexts using sums and differences.
description_plain: Your child can use models to add and subtract whole numbers from -20 to 20 and explain real-life situations using these sums and differences.

subject: Math
description: Understand subtraction of integers as adding the additive inverse, p − q = p + (– q). Show that the distance between two integers on the number line is the absolute value of their difference.
description_plain: Your child understands that subtracting a number is the same as adding its opposite and can find the distance between two numbers on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Understand p + q as the number located a distance q from p, in the positive or negative direction depending on the sign of q. Show that a number and its additive inverse create a zero pair.
description_plain: Your child understands adding numbers as moving along the number line and knows that a number plus its opposite equals zero.

subject: Math
description: Describe situations in which opposite quantities combine to make 0.
description_plain: Your child can describe situations where two opposite amounts combine to make zero.

subject: Math
description: Find and position pairs of rational numbers on a coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can find and plot pairs of numbers on a coordinate grid.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that when two ordered pairs differ only by signs, the locations of the points are related by reflections across one or both axes.
description_plain: Your child knows that points with signs changed in their pairs are reflections across the axes on a coordinate plane.

subject: Math
description: Understand signs of numbers in ordered pairs as indicating locations in quadrants.
description_plain: Your child understands that the signs in number pairs tell where points are located in the four parts of the coordinate plane.

subject: Math
description: Find and position rational numbers on a horizontal or vertical number line.
description_plain: Your child can find and place rational numbers on a number line going left to right or up and down.

subject: Math
description: Recognize opposite signs of numbers as indicating locations on opposite sides of 0 and that the opposite of the opposite of a number is the number itself.
description_plain: Your child knows that numbers with opposite signs are on opposite sides of zero and that the opposite of the opposite of a number is the number itself.

subject: Math
description: Distinguish comparisons of absolute value from statements about order.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between comparing absolute values and comparing which number is bigger or smaller.

subject: Math
description: Interpret absolute value as magnitude for a positive or negative quantity in a real-world context.
description_plain: Your child understands absolute value as how big a number is, no matter if it’s positive or negative, in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Understand the absolute value of a rational number as its distance from 0 on the number line to:
description_plain: Your child understands absolute value as the distance a number is from zero on the number line.

subject: Math
description: Represent quantities in real-world contexts, explaining the meaning of 0 in each situation.
description_plain: Your child can show real-life amounts and explain what zero means in each case.

subject: Math
description: Describe quantities having opposite directions or values.
description_plain: Your child can describe amounts that go in opposite directions or have opposite values.

subject: Math
description: Find the least common multiple of two whole numbers less than or equal to 12 to add and subtract fractions with unlike denominators.
description_plain: Your child can find the smallest common multiple of two numbers up to 12 to help add and subtract fractions with different bottom numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use the greatest common factor and the distributive property to rewrite the sum of two whole numbers, each less than or equal to 100.
description_plain: Your child can use the greatest common factor and distributive property to rewrite the sum of two whole numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Find the greatest common factor of two whole numbers less than or equal to 100.
description_plain: Your child can find the greatest common factor of two whole numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Find the unique prime factorization for a whole number.
description_plain: Your child can break down a whole number into its unique prime factors.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving division of fractions.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems that involve dividing fractions.

subject: Math
description: Interpret and compute quotients of fractions.
description_plain: Your child can understand and calculate the result of dividing fractions.

subject: Math
description: Finding the whole, given a part and the percent.
description_plain: Your child can find the whole amount when given a part and the percent it represents.

subject: Math
description: Using equivalent ratios, such as benchmark percents (50%, 25%, 10%, 5%, 1%), to determine a part of any given quantity.
description_plain: Your child can use common percent benchmarks like 50%, 25%, 10%, 5%, and 1% to find parts of any amount.

subject: Math
description: Understanding and finding a percent of a quantity as a ratio per 100.
description_plain: Your child understands what a percent means and can find a percent of a number.

subject: Math
description: Plotting the pairs of values on the coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can plot pairs of numbers on a coordinate plane.

subject: Math
description: Converting and manipulating measurements using given ratios.
description_plain: Your child can change and work with measurements using given ratios.

subject: Math
description: Using a unit ratio.
description_plain: Using a unit ratio.

subject: Math
description: Finding missing values in the tables.
description_plain: Your child can find missing numbers in ratio tables.

subject: Math
description: Creating and using a table to compare ratios.
description_plain: Your child can make and use tables to compare ratios.

subject: Math
description: Model a ratio relationship using a variety of representations.
description_plain: Your child can show ratio relationships using different ways like pictures, numbers, or words.

subject: Math
description: Describe a ratio as a multiplicative relationship between two quantities.
description_plain: Your child understands that a ratio compares two quantities by multiplication.

description: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to explain how instruments that transmit and detect waves are used in everyday life.
description_plain: Your child can find, understand, and explain how devices that send and receive waves are used in everyday life.

description_plain: Your child can find, understand, and explain how devices that send and receive waves are used in everyday life.

description: Carry out investigations to explain the quantitative and qualitative relationships among wave frequency, wave velocity, wavelength, and wave energy.
description_plain: Your child can do experiments to explain how wave frequency, speed, length, and energy are related.

description: Analyze the nature of waves and their applications.
description_plain: Your child can study waves and understand how they are used in real life.

description_plain: Your child can explain how work and power are related, both by numbers and by ideas.

subject: Science
description: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to explain the impacts on humans and mitigation strategies of potentially hazardous environmental factors (including air quality index, UV index, Heat Index, Wildfires) and storms (hurricanes, blizzards, tornadoes, severe thunderstorms, floods).
description_plain: Your child can find and share information about how things like air quality, UV rays, heat, wildfires, and storms affect people and how to stay safe.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to explain how changes in the structure and composition of the atmosphere affects the greenhouse effect and global temperatures.
description_plain: Your child can study data to explain how changes in the air affect the greenhouse effect and Earth's temperature.

subject: Science
description: Engage in argument from evidence to explain that the good health of humans and environment requires: monitoring of the atmosphere, maintaining air quality and stewardship.
description_plain: Your child can use evidence to explain why keeping the air clean and watching the atmosphere is important for people and the environment.

subject: Science
description: Understand the reciprocal relationship between the atmosphere and humans.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and the atmosphere affect each other.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain the influence of convection, global winds, and the jet stream on weather and climatic conditions.
description_plain: Your child can use models to explain how things like convection, global winds, and the jet stream affect weather and climate.

subject: Science
description: Use models to predict weather conditions based on observations (including clouds, air masses, fronts), measurements (wind speed and direction, air temperature, humidity and air pressure), weather maps, satellites and radar.
description_plain: Your child can use models and weather information to predict weather conditions.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to explain the relationship between the movement of air masses, high and low pressure systems, frontal boundaries and weather conditions that may result.
description_plain: Your child can study data to explain how moving air, pressure systems, and fronts cause different weather.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain how the energy of the Sun and Earth’s gravity drive the cycling of water, including changes of state, as it moves through multiple pathways in Earth’s systems and relates to weather patterns on Earth.
description_plain: Your child can use models to explain how the Sun's energy and Earth's gravity move water through the environment and affect weather.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to compare the composition, properties and structure of Earth’s atmosphere to include: mixtures of gases and differences in temperature and pressure within layers.
description_plain: Your child can compare data to explain what the air is made of and how temperature and pressure change in different layers.

subject: Science
description: Understand the atmosphere and how the cycling of water relates to Earth’s weather and climate.
description_plain: Your child understands the atmosphere and how water cycles relate to weather and climate.

subject: Science
description: Use models (Punnett squares) to infer and predict patterns of the inheritance of single genetic traits from parent to offspring (including dominant and recessive traits).
description_plain: Your child can use Punnett squares to predict how traits from parents might show up in their children.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain how asexual reproduction results in offspring with identical genetic information while sexual reproduction results in offspring with genetic variation (not to include specific phases of mitosis and meiosis).
description_plain: Your child can explain how asexual reproduction makes identical offspring and sexual reproduction creates offspring with differences.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation supported with scientific evidence to summarize the role of genes on chromosomes as inherited cellular structures which contribute to an organism's traits (not to include the structure of DNA).
description_plain: Your child can explain how genes on chromosomes carry inherited traits from parents to offspring.

subject: Science
description: Understand the relationship of the mechanisms of reproduction, patterns of inheritance, and potential variation among offspring.
description_plain: Your child understands how reproduction and inheritance work together to create differences in offspring.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation to summarize how the major systems of the human body interact with each other to support life (including digestion, respiration, reproduction, circulation, excretion, nervous).
description_plain: Your child can explain how major body systems work together to keep a person alive.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain how the hierarchical organization of multicellular organisms from cells to tissues to organs to systems to organisms functions to support life.
description_plain: Your child can use models to show how cells group into tissues, organs, and systems to support life.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain how the relevant structures within cells (including cell membrane, cell wall, nucleus, mitochondria, chloroplasts, and vacuoles) function to support the life of plant, animal, and bacterial cells.
description_plain: Your child can explain how parts of plant, animal, and bacterial cells help them live.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation to conclude how the structures of single-celled organisms carry out all of the basic functions of life including: Euglena, Amoeba, Paramecium, Volvox.
description_plain: Your child can explain how single-celled organisms carry out all life functions.

subject: Science
description: Understand the processes, structures and functions of living organisms that enable them to survive, reproduce and carry out the basic functions of life.
description_plain: Your child understands how living things survive, reproduce, and do basic life activities.

subject: Science
description: Carry out investigations to compare the efficiency of simple machines in relation to their advantages for particular purposes (to include inclined planes, pulleys, levers and wheel and axles) using qualitative data.
description_plain: Your child can do experiments to compare how well simple machines like levers and pulleys work for different jobs.

subject: Science
description: Carry out investigations to conclude that energy can be transferred from one system to another when two objects push or pull on each other over a distance (work) in a mechanical system using qualitative data.
description_plain: Your child can do experiments to show how energy moves when objects push or pull each other.

subject: Science
description: Engage in argument from evidence to explain how energy can be transformed from one form to another, specifically potential energy and kinetic energy (models could include roller coasters, pendulums, or cars on ramps as examples).
description_plain: Your child can explain how energy changes from stored energy to moving energy using examples like roller coasters or pendulums.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation to summarize how kinetic and potential energy contribute to the mechanical energy of an object.
description_plain: Your child can explain how moving energy and stored energy work together to make an object move.

subject: Science
description: Understand forms of energy, energy transfer and transformation, and conservation in mechanical systems.
description_plain: Your child understands different types of energy, how energy moves and changes, and that energy is not lost in mechanical systems.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret graphical data to summarize the motion of an object to show a change in distance over a period of time for constant speed and variable motion.
description_plain: Your child can read graphs to describe how far an object moves over time when it moves at a steady or changing speed.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret graphical data to summarize the motion of an object to show a change in position over a period of time.
description_plain: Your child can read graphs to explain how an object's position changes over time.

subject: Science
description: Use models to illustrate the effects of balanced and unbalanced forces acting on an object (including friction, gravity, and magnetism).
description_plain: Your child can use models to show how balanced and unbalanced forces like friction, gravity, and magnets affect how objects move.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation to summarize the motion of an object by its position, direction of motion, and speed in respect to some other object.
description_plain: Your child can explain how an object moves by describing its position, direction, and speed compared to something else.

subject: Science
description: Understand motion, the effects of forces on motion, and the graphical representations of motion.
description_plain: Your child understands how objects move, how forces affect that movement, and how to read graphs about motion.

subject: Science
description: Obtain, evaluate, and communicate information to compare the long term implications of the use of renewable and nonrenewable energy resources and the importance of stewardship and conservation.
description_plain: Your child can find and share information comparing the long-term effects of using renewable and nonrenewable energy and why saving energy matters.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to illustrate the relationship between human activities and global temperatures since industrialization.
description_plain: Your child can study data to show how human activities have affected global temperatures since the industrial age.

subject: Science
description: Engage in argument from evidence to explain the environmental consequences of the various methods of obtaining, transforming, and distributing energy.
description_plain: Your child can explain, using evidence, the environmental effects of different ways we get and use energy.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation to classify the primary sources of energy as either renewable (Geothermal, Biomass, Solar, Wind, Hydroelectric) or nonrenewable (Coal, Petroleum, Natural Gas, Nuclear).
description_plain: Your child can explain which energy sources are renewable like solar and wind, and which are nonrenewable like coal and oil.

subject: Science
description: Understand the environmental implications associated with the various methods of obtaining, managing, and using energy resources.
description_plain: Your child understands how getting and using energy affects the environment.

subject: Science
description: Engage in argument from evidence to explain that the good health of humans and the environment requires: monitoring of the hydrosphere, water quality standards, methods of water treatment, maintaining safe water quality, and stewardship.
description_plain: Your child can explain why keeping water clean and safe is important for people and the environment.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to predict the safety and potability of water supplies in North Carolina based on physical and biological factors, including: temperature, dissolved oxygen, pH, nitrates and phosphates, turbidity, and bio-indicators.
description_plain: Your child can look at data to decide if water in North Carolina is safe to drink based on things like temperature and pollution.

subject: Science
description: Understand the reciprocal relationship between the hydrosphere and humans.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and water systems affect each other.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain how temperature and salinity drive major ocean currents and how these currents impact climate, ecosystems, and the distribution of nutrients, minerals, dissolved gases, and life forms.
description_plain: Your child can use models to show how ocean currents move because of temperature and salt, and how these currents affect climate and life.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain the structure of the hydrosphere including: water distribution on earth, local river basins, estuaries, and water availability.
description_plain: Your child can use models to explain where Earth's water is found and how local rivers and estuaries work.

subject: Science
description: Understand the hydrosphere including freshwater, estuarine, ocean systems.
description_plain: Your child understands Earth's water systems, including fresh water, estuaries, and oceans.

subject: Science
description: Understand the history of Earth and its life forms based on evidence of change recorded in fossil records and landforms.
description_plain: Your child understands Earth's history and how life has changed by looking at fossils and land features.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain the process of natural selection, in which genetic variations in a population affect individuals’ likelihood of surviving and reproducing in its environment.
description_plain: Your child can use models to explain how differences in genes help some animals survive and have babies better than others.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to infer evolutionary relationships by using evidence drawn from fossils and comparative anatomy.
description_plain: Your child can study fossils and body parts to figure out how different living things are related.

subject: Science
description: Use models to explain how the flow of energy within food webs is interconnected with the cycling of matter (water and carbon).
description_plain: Your child can use models to show how energy moves through food chains and how water and carbon cycle through ecosystems.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation to summarize how food provides the energy and the building materials required for the growth and survival of all organisms (to include plants).
description_plain: Your child can explain how food gives energy and materials that all living things, including plants, need to grow and survive.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation to summarize the relationships among producers, consumers, and decomposers including the positive and negative consequences of such interactions including: coexistence and cooperation, competition (predator/prey), parasitism, and mutualism.
description_plain: Your child can explain how producers, consumers, and decomposers interact in nature, including helpful and harmful relationships.

subject: Science
description: Carry out investigations to explain how changing biotic and abiotic factors such as food, water, shelter, and space affect populations in an ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child can investigate how changes in food, water, shelter, and space affect animal populations in an ecosystem.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to explain the difference between epidemic and pandemic as it relates to the spread, treatment and prevention of disease.
description_plain: Your child can study data to explain the difference between an epidemic and a pandemic and how diseases spread and are treated.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation to compare the basic characteristics of viruses, bacteria, fungi and parasites relating to the spread, treatment and prevention of disease.
description_plain: Your child can explain the differences between viruses, bacteria, fungi, and parasites and how they spread and can be prevented or treated.

subject: Science
description: Understand the hazards caused by agents of diseases that affect living organisms.
description_plain: Your child understands how germs and other disease-causing agents can harm living things.

subject: Science
description: Use models to illustrate how atoms are rearranged during a chemical reaction so that balanced chemical equations support the Law of Conservation of Mass (in both open and closed systems).
description_plain: Your child can use models to show how atoms rearrange during chemical reactions and how mass stays the same.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation to classify changes in matter as physical changes (including changes in size, shape, and state) or chemical changes that are the result of a chemical reaction (including changes in energy, color, formation of a gas or precipitate).
description_plain: Your child can explain the difference between physical changes like melting and chemical changes like color change or gas forming.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to explain how the physical properties of elements and their reactivity have been used to produce the current model of the Periodic Table of Elements.
description_plain: Your child can study data to explain how elements' properties helped create the Periodic Table.

subject: Science
description: Use models to illustrate the structure of atoms in terms of the protons, electrons, and neutrons (using the location, charges and comparative size of these subatomic particles), without consideration of isotopes, ions, and energy levels.
description_plain: Your child can use models to show the parts of an atom: protons, electrons, and neutrons, and their charges and sizes.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation to classify matter as elements, compounds, or mixtures based on how the atoms are arranged in various substances.
description_plain: Your child can explain how matter is made of elements, compounds, or mixtures based on how atoms are arranged.

subject: Science
description: Understand the properties of matter and changes that occur when matter interacts in open and closed systems.
description_plain: Your child understands the properties of matter and what happens when matter changes in different systems.
